sast_review.reason: `file_path = os.path.join(UPLOAD_DIR, sanitized_filename)` can escape the directory because `sanitize_filename` does not remove '..' or '/'. An attacker can write files outside `avatars/`.
`os.remove(os.path.join(UPLOAD_DIR, sanitized_filename))` uses the same vulnerable path, allowing arbitrary file deletion.
`file_path` is constructed from user-controlled `username` via `sanitize_filename(username)`, which does not prevent path traversal. Allows checking existence of arbitrary files.
Same `file_path` as line 55 is opened for reading, enabling arbitrary file read.
Same `file_path` is opened again for reading, enabling arbitrary file read.

raw.summary: All 5 SAST findings are true positives. The sanitize_filename function does not remove path separators ('/' or '..'), so all uses of user-controlled filenames (lines 35, 41, 55, 59, 70) are vulnerable to path traversal (CWE-22). Additionally, the multipart parsing is fundamentally broken (incorrect boundary handling, file data extraction), and the MIME check on line 35 writes the file before validating it, creating a time-of-check-time-of-use issue. The MIME validation logic is also broken: it compares raw bytes to MIME type strings, which will always fail, so all uploads will be rejected or the file written and then deleted.
